faqs.q: What is the difference between acute stress disorder and PTSD?
faqs.a: Both involve intense reactions to trauma, like flashbacks, avoidance, and being on high alert. The main difference is timing. Acute stress disorder is diagnosed in the early window after a trauma, roughly a few days up to about a month, while PTSD is considered when symptoms continue beyond that window.

faqs.q: Does acute stress disorder always turn into PTSD?
faqs.a: No. Having acute stress disorder doesn't mean a person will definitely go on to develop PTSD. Many people recover as the early period passes, especially with support and trauma-focused care.

faqs.q: How is acute stress disorder treated?
faqs.a: Support and trauma-focused therapy in the early window can ease distress and may lower the chance that symptoms harden into longer-term difficulties. Reaching out early can help, particularly if you can't function or the distress is severe.
